questions.question: What is a TTL really expressing in a cached system?
options: A maximum tolerated stale window for that data class under normal operation.
A guarantee that the cache and source are always identical.
The number of regions serving the object.
The size of the object in bytes.
questions.explanation: TTL is a freshness and load trade-off, not just a random duration.

questions.question: Why is explicit invalidation often added on top of TTL rather than replacing it completely?
options: Because TTL can still limit damage if an invalidation event is missed or delayed.
Because TTL makes versions unnecessary.
Because invalidation only works in browsers.
Because caches cannot store timestamps.
questions.explanation: TTL often acts as a safety net when event-driven freshness is imperfect.

questions.question: When is explicit invalidation most worth its complexity?
options: When freshness soon after writes materially changes user-visible correctness or policy behavior.
When content never changes.
When origin traffic is already negligible.
When you want to avoid naming key ownership.
questions.explanation: Explicit invalidation is justified when stale reads near writes are meaningfully harmful.

questions.question: What is the main operational danger of broad prefix invalidation?
options: It can destroy the working set and trigger a large miss storm.
It guarantees data corruption at the source of truth.
It makes responses uncompressible.
It disables tracing.
questions.explanation: Large invalidations can collapse hit ratio and flood the origin even when correctness is fine.

questions.question: Which metric best detects freshness-path trouble?
options: Invalidation publish-to-apply latency or version skew.
Only process uptime.
The number of SREs on the team.
Average markdown heading length.
questions.explanation: Propagation delay and version mismatch directly reveal freshness drift.

questions.question: Why are version-aware reads useful?
options: They let readers reason about whether cached data is recent enough instead of treating values as opaque.
They eliminate all misses automatically.
They force caches to become durable databases.
They make TTL unnecessary in every system.
questions.explanation: Version metadata makes stale behavior measurable and sometimes selectively avoidable.

questions.question: Which interview statement is strongest?
options: I will set one global 5-minute TTL for simplicity and avoid discussing user-visible stale windows.
I will classify data by mutability and user impact, then mix TTL and explicit invalidation where the product promise demands it.
I will invalidate every key on every write regardless of scale.
I will avoid freshness discussion until after the architecture diagram is approved.
questions.explanation: Strong answers tie freshness mechanisms to data classes and consequences.

questions.question: What hidden trade-off usually comes with tighter freshness?
options: More invalidation traffic, more misses, or more coordination cost against the origin.
Lower storage use and less origin work automatically.
No change, because freshness is free once a cache exists.
Fewer observability requirements.
questions.explanation: Stronger freshness generally costs more in coordination, traffic, or cache churn.
